summary: I graduated from Kiowa High School with a class of 31 students, most of whom started kindergarten together. One of my favorite teachers there was the industrial arts teacher, Mr. McKinley, who inspired me to build the desk I'm sitting at now, which I made when I was 15. My father also worked as the janitor at the school for a time, which showed his dedication to our education and future.

After leaving Oologah, I moved to Claremore, Oklahoma, where I became a superintendent. We initially leased a house for a year before building our own home, where all my kids lived and graduated from Claremore High School. Claremore has been my home for many years now, and I even moved my mother into assisted living there so she could be part of our lives at the end.

Before moving to Claremore, I lived in Oologah, Oklahoma. It was the place I left when I took the superintendent position in Claremore. My career in education took me through various towns in Oklahoma, and Oologah was an important stop before settling in Claremore.

In February, my wife and I traveled to Sweden to visit our youngest son and his three children, including a new baby born in mid-January. My son is the principal of the International English School in Örebro, Sweden. We went on a challenging hike with him and his older kids, and I was in great shape at the time, having recently walked 180 consecutive days of 10,000 steps or more.

My youngest son is the principal of the International English School in Örebro, Sweden. We visited him there in February to see his new baby, and it was a wonderful family trip. The school is where he works and leads, and it was great to see him thriving in that role overseas.

I have a deep connection with the University of Oklahoma, where I studied speech and psychology as a double major during my undergraduate years. I also saw several doctors affiliated with OU when I began experiencing health issues. Dr. Beasley, one of my doctors there, was very concerned and supportive throughout my diagnosis process.

After seeing multiple doctors and inconclusive tests, I was referred to the Mayo Clinic in September. I was surprised to get an appointment there, but with the help of my wife and doctors, I was able to go. I saw specialists there in September and October, and it was at Mayo Clinic that I finally received my diagnosis in December.

In December, I was diagnosed with Lou Gehrig's disease, also known as ALS. This was the biggest challenge of my life, as I began to experience weakness and balance issues starting in March after returning from Sweden. The disease has progressed to the point where I now require a walker and am working with specialists in Oklahoma City to manage symptoms, including possible brain degeneration.

Recently, I started seeing an ALS specialist in Oklahoma City who suspects some brain degeneration is complicating my condition. This specialist is helping me manage my Lou Gehrig's disease symptoms. Oklahoma City has become an important place for my ongoing medical care.

I was once the superintendent of Tulsa Public Schools, a role I took great pride in. My background in speech and psychology helped me in that leadership position. My work in Tulsa was significant enough that my doctors wrote a letter about it to assist with my Mayo Clinic referral.

My mother grew up on a farm near Hazelton, Kansas, where she was one of nine siblings. Hazelton was just six miles from Kiowa, Kansas, where I grew up. The farm life there was active with cattle and horses, and I spent time working on that farm as a child.

I grew up in Kiowa, Kansas, just six miles from Hazelton. My parents moved there to put my sister and me in Kiowa schools, where I started kindergarten. It was a close-knit community where my father worked various jobs, including janitor at the school I attended. Despite growing up poor, Kiowa was a great place to grow up with strong family and church values.

My mother, Norma Jean Gates Ballard, grew up on a farm near Hazelton, Kansas, with eight siblings. She was very active as a child, especially riding horses. She was a terrific, warm, and loving mother who played a big role in our close-knit family and supported our strong church involvement.

My father, William Edward Ballard, was an extraordinarily bright man with a very high IQ. Despite his intelligence, he worked whatever jobs he could find in Kiowa, including being the janitor at my school, to provide for us. He was a leader in the church and deeply dedicated to his family, emphasizing education and college from our earliest days.

My older sister, Connie Jean Ballard, was a wonderful older sibling and the 'commander of the corps' in our family. She started as a home economics teacher and later became a librarian, eventually moving to Topeka, Kansas. We remained extremely close until she passed away from cancer a few years ago, just before I took over at Tulsa Public Schools.

My younger brother, William Joseph Ballard, known as Joe, was very close to me and a talented athlete who could have had scholarships in football or basketball. Sadly, he died of cancer at age 20, which was a monumental moment in my life. On his deathbed, he encouraged me to live well and do my best, which has motivated me throughout my life.

At the time my brother was dying, I was teaching in Coweta, Oklahoma. We went home for the weekend to be with him during his final days. That period was very impactful for me and strengthened the bond I felt with my siblings and family.

My sister Connie Jean eventually moved to Topeka, Kansas, where she worked as a librarian. Despite the distance, we remained extremely close until her passing. Topeka became her home later in life after starting her career as a home economics teacher.
